lessons.title: Unlocking Trading Insights with AI: A Beginner's Guide to RAG Pipelines
lessons.tagline: Discover how to run a basic RAG pipeline locally with Ollama and ChromaDB, and unlock the power of AI-driven trading document analysis
lessons.showNotes:
In this episode, we'll delve into the world of AI and machine learning, exploring how to set up a basic RAG pipeline using Ollama and ChromaDB. We'll discuss the benefits of using this technology to analyze trading documents, and provide a step-by-step guide on how to get started. From understanding the core concepts to applying them in real-world scenarios, we'll cover it all. 
The use of RAG pipelines has been gaining traction in recent years, with companies like Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase leveraging this technology to improve their trading operations. By the end of this episode, you'll have a solid understanding of how to run a basic RAG pipeline locally and be able to apply this knowledge to your own trading document analysis

segments.label: Cold Open
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: According to a report by McKinsey, the use of AI and machine learning in trading operations can result in a 10-20% increase in revenue. However, many beginners are unsure of where to start when it comes to implementing these technologies. In 2020, the trading platform, Robinhood, experienced a 30% increase in trading volume, resulting in a significant surge in trading documents. To effectively analyze these documents, companies are turning to RAG pipelines, which utilize natural language processing to extract insights and improve trading decisions. In this episode, we'll explore how to set up a basic RAG pipeline using Ollama and ChromaDB, and discuss the benefits of using this technology in trading document analysis.

segments.label: Core Concept
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: So, how does a RAG pipeline work? At its core, a RAG pipeline consists of three main components: retrieval, aggregation, and generation. The retrieval component is responsible for fetching relevant data from a database, such as ChromaDB. The aggregation component then processes this data, using techniques like entity recognition and sentiment analysis. Finally, the generation component uses the aggregated data to produce insights and predictions. Ollama is a popular framework for building RAG pipelines, providing a range of pre-built components and tools to simplify the development process. With Ollama, you can easily integrate your RAG pipeline with ChromaDB, and start analyzing trading documents in no time. For example, you can use Ollama to build a RAG pipeline that extracts insights from trading news articles, and then uses this data to inform trading decisions.

segments.label: Real-World Example
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: Let's take a look at a real-world example of how a RAG pipeline can be used in trading document analysis. On January 28, 2020, the trading platform, eToro, announced a partnership with the AI startup, Finimize. As part of this partnership, eToro used Finimize's RAG pipeline to analyze trading documents and provide personalized investment recommendations to its users. The results were impressive, with eToro reporting a 25% increase in user engagement and a 15% increase in trading volume. This example demonstrates the power of RAG pipelines in trading document analysis, and highlights the potential benefits of using this technology in your own trading operations.

segments.label: Deep Dive
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: Now that we've covered the basics of RAG pipelines, let's dive deeper into the technical details. One of the key challenges of building a RAG pipeline is ensuring that the retrieval component is able to effectively fetch relevant data from the database. This requires a deep understanding of the database schema and the structure of the trading documents. Additionally, the aggregation component must be able to handle large volumes of data, and produce accurate insights and predictions. To overcome these challenges, practitioners often use techniques like data preprocessing, feature engineering, and model selection. For example, you can use data preprocessing to remove noise and irrelevant data from the trading documents, and then use feature engineering to extract relevant features from the preprocessed data. By using these techniques, you can build a robust and effective RAG pipeline that produces accurate insights and predictions.

segments.label: Key Takeaways
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: In summary, running a basic RAG pipeline locally with Ollama and ChromaDB is a powerful way to analyze trading documents and unlock the power of AI-driven insights. By understanding the core concepts of RAG pipelines, and applying them in real-world scenarios, you can improve your trading operations and stay ahead of the competition. The key takeaways from this episode are: RAG pipelines consist of retrieval, aggregation, and generation components; Ollama is a popular framework for building RAG pipelines; and ChromaDB is a powerful database for storing and managing trading documents.

segments.label: Next Steps
segments.speaker: Host
segments.script: If you're interested in learning more about RAG pipelines and how to apply them in your own trading operations, I recommend checking out the Ollama documentation and the ChromaDB website. Additionally, you can explore the Finimize website to learn more about their AI-powered trading platform. By following these resources, you can start building your own RAG pipeline and unlocking the power of AI-driven trading insights.

quiz.q: What is the primary function of the retrieval component in a RAG pipeline?
opts: To aggregate data and produce features
To fetch relevant data from a database
To preprocess and clean the data
quiz.explain: The retrieval component is responsible for fetching relevant data from a database, such as ChromaDB, to be used in the RAG pipeline.

quiz.q: Which framework is commonly used for building RAG pipelines?
quiz.explain: Ollama is a popular framework for building RAG pipelines, providing a range of pre-built components and tools to simplify the development process.

quiz.q: What was the outcome of eToro's partnership with Finimize in 2020?
opts: A 25% increase in user engagement and a 15% increase in trading volume
A 20% increase in user engagement and a 10% decrease in trading volume
quiz.explain: The partnership between eToro and Finimize resulted in a 25% increase in user engagement and a 15% increase in trading volume.

quiz.q: What is the primary challenge of building a RAG pipeline?
opts: Ensuring the generation component produces accurate insights and predictions
Ensuring the aggregation component can handle large volumes of data
Ensuring the retrieval component can effectively fetch relevant data from the database
Ensuring the database is properly configured
quiz.explain: The primary challenge of building a RAG pipeline is ensuring that the retrieval component can effectively fetch relevant data from the database, which requires a deep understanding of the database schema and the structure of the trading documents.

quiz.q: What is the name of the database used in the example?
quiz.explain: The database used in the example is ChromaDB, which is a powerful database for storing and managing trading documents.

planData.title: LLM & RAG Optimization for Local Algo Trading
planData.summary: A structured plan to optimize a local algo trading system using ChromaDB and Ollama with qwen2.5:7b, progressing from basic RAG setup to production-level latency and reliability.

belts.objective: Run a basic RAG pipeline locally with Ollama and ChromaDB, querying trading documents.
belts.project: Set up Ollama with qwen2.5:7b, create a ChromaDB collection of 10 trading documents, and run a query with context from the top-3 documents.
belts.checkpoint: You can successfully query the local LLM and receive answers that include relevant info from your documents.

belts.objective: Optimize chunking and embedding strategies to improve retrieval precision for trading data.

resources.title: Best Embedding Models for Code and Finance

belts.project: Experiment with chunk sizes 128, 256, 512 tokens and two embedding models (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2 vs. bge-base-en-v1.5) on a set of 50 trading reports, measuring recall@5.
belts.checkpoint: You can choose the best chunk size and embedding model for your domain based on recall metrics.

belts.objective: Implement hybrid search (dense + sparse) and metadata filtering for time-aware retrieval.
belts.project: Add BM25 sparse retrieval to ChromaDB (via separate index or external library) and filter by date field (e.g., only docs from last 30 days). Benchmark latency and accuracy.
belts.checkpoint: Your system returns relevant trading context in under 1 second and the top results respect date filters.

belts.objective: Apply cross-encoder reranking and query transformation to boost answer quality.
belts.project: Integrate a cross-encoder reranker (e.g., BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3) after retrieval and try query expansion (e.g., generate 2 paraphrase queries). Evaluate on a test set of 20 trading questions.
belts.checkpoint: Your answer accuracy (manual or automated) improves by at least 10% over the baseline without reranking.

belts.objective: Fine-tune the LLM (qwen2.5:7b) with LoRA on domain-specific trading data for better generation.
belts.project: Collect 200 question-answer pairs from your trading logs and fine-tune qwen2.5:7b using LoRA (rank=8). Compare generated summaries before and after fine-tuning.
belts.checkpoint: You can produce trading-specific outputs (e.g., market summaries) that are more concise and accurate than the base model.

belts.objective: Productionize the optimized RAG pipeline with caching, concurrency, and monitoring for low-latency live trading.

resources.title: Optimizing LLM Inference for Real-time Systems

belts.project: Implement response caching (e.g., Redis), async request handling (e.g., AsyncIO or FastAPI), and a monitoring dashboard (e.g., Prometheus + Grafana) for query latency and cache hit rate.
belts.checkpoint: Your system can handle 10+ queries per second with p95 latency under 500ms and you can observe throughput in a dashboard.

tips: Start with a small dataset (5-10 documents) to iterate quickly on chunking and embeddings before scaling up.
Use evaluation metrics like recall@k, mean reciprocal rank (MRR), and end-to-end answer correctness (e.g., LLM-as-judge).
Monitor token usage and inference time early; bottlenecks often shift from retrieval to LLM generation as you optimize.
